source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: Gemini 3 confirmed pre-training scaling laws remain intact, which is bullish for next-generation model quality on Blackwell hardware
summary_extended: Baker argues that Gemini 3 served as the first meaningful validation of pre-training scaling laws since the Hopper generation, and the fact that those laws held is a foundational confirmation for the entire AI capital expenditure bull case. He contends this is not merely additive but multiplicative in effect, because it compounds with two newly established post-training scaling laws — RLVR and test-time compute — meaning models trained on Blackwell hardware will be dramatically more capable than those trained on Hopper. The investment implication is straightforwardly bullish for Nvidia, as sustained pre-training scaling laws justify continued and accelerating demand for the most advanced GPU clusters, with Blackwell being the direct beneficiary of this validation. Baker frames Gemini 3's confirmation as removing a key uncertainty that had hung over the AI infrastructure thesis, making the case for sustained hyperscaler GPU procurement significantly stronger.
detail: Baker argues Gemini 3 was the first real test of pre-training scaling laws since Hopper, and it held. This is multiplicative with the two new post-training scaling laws (RLVR and test-time compute), meaning Blackwell-trained models will be dramatically better than Hopper-trained ones.
confidence_evidence: Baker states 'scaling laws for pre-training are intact' and calls Gemini 3 'very important' for confirming this; frames it as foundational to the entire bull case for AI capex
quote_fragment: Gemini 3 was very important because it showed scaling laws for pre-training are intact

ticker_audit.reasoning: Sustained pre-training scaling laws directly justify continued large-scale GPU procurement; Blackwell is Nvidia's current flagship training hardware, making Nvidia the primary commercial beneficiary of this thesis.

Gemini 3 is Google's model and is the specific empirical evidence Baker cites to make this claim; Google is the direct subject of the scaling law validation, though the implication extends beyond Google.
